content.text: Server-Sent Events (SSE) is the standard transport for streaming LLM responses to a browser. Unlike WebSockets, SSE is a unidirectional HTTP connection: the server pushes chunks as they arrive from the model, and the client appends them to the UI in real time. The browser handles automatic reconnection, and the protocol fits naturally on top of standard HTTP/2 infrastructure.

attrs.alt: SSE LLM streaming architecture: browser ReadableStream → FastAPI StreamingResponse → Anthropic stream

content.text: SSE is a plain-text protocol over a persistent HTTP response with Content-Type: text/event-stream. Each message is one or more field lines followed by a blank line.

# Minimal data event
data: {"delta": "Hello, world!"}

# Named event type
event: error
data: {"code": "rate_limit", "message": "Too many requests"}

# With ID and retry hint
id: msg_01Xa3
retry: 3000
data: {"delta": "next token"}

# Sentinel — stream complete
data: [DONE]

The blank line (double newline) terminates each event and triggers the browser's onmessage callback. A missing blank line means the message is not dispatched yet — partial lines are buffered by the browser and accumulated until the delimiter arrives.
Multiple data: lines are joined with \n before dispatch
Defaults to 'message'; client listens with addEventListener(type, ...)
Sent as Last-Event-ID header on reconnect
Browser uses this value if the connection drops
Ignored by EventSource; useful to prevent proxy timeouts
accepts an async generator and sets the correct headers automatically. The generator yields formatted SSE strings; the client receives them as they are produced.
header is essential when nginx sits in front of FastAPI — without it nginx buffers the entire response before forwarding, defeating streaming.
API does not support POST requests or custom headers, so use
with a ReadableStream reader instead. This gives full control over request headers (Authorization, Content-Type) and the body.
lines for structured errors. The fetch loop above reads raw SSE text, so you must parse event type from the raw lines:
API reconnects automatically and sends the last received event ID in a
header. To support resumable streams with the fetch approach, you manage IDs explicitly.

# Backend: emit ID on each chunk
async def _stream_response(req: ChatRequest, user_id: str):
    event_id = 0
    async with client.messages.stream(...) as stream:
        async for text_delta in stream.text_stream:
            event_id += 1
            yield f"id: {event_id}\n"
            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'delta': text_delta})}\n\n"

For LLM streaming, resumption is only useful if the server stores partial responses server-side (e.g., in Redis). Most simple implementations simply restart the generation from the beginning on reconnect, which is usually acceptable since responses complete in seconds. Full resumption is valuable for document-length generation jobs.

Cloud load balancers and proxies kill idle HTTP connections after 30–60 seconds. During model thinking time (especially with long context or reasoning models), the server may not emit tokens for that long. Send SSE comments as heartbeats:

import asyncio

async def _stream_with_keepalive(req: ChatRequest, user_id: str):
    stream_gen = _stream_response(req, user_id)
    keepalive_task = None

    async def keepalive():
        while True:
            await asyncio.sleep(20)
            yield ": keep-alive\n\n"

    # Use asyncio.Queue to merge two async generators
    queue: asyncio.Queue[str | None] = asyncio.Queue()

    async def _produce():
        async for chunk in stream_gen:
            await queue.put(chunk)
        await queue.put(None)   # sentinel

    async def _heartbeat():
        while True:
            await asyncio.sleep(20)
            await queue.put(": keep-alive\n\n")

    producer = asyncio.create_task(_produce())
    heartbeat = asyncio.create_task(_heartbeat())

    try:
        while True:
            item = await queue.get()
            if item is None:
                break
            yield item
    finally:
        heartbeat.cancel()
        await asyncio.gather(producer, heartbeat, return_exceptions=True)
Standard HTTP headers on initial request
Only on handshake; custom headers need workarounds
For text-generation chat, SSE is almost always the right choice. WebSockets add complexity with no benefit when the client sends a request and the server streams back exactly one response.

# tests/test_sse_stream.py
import json, pytest
from backend.routes.chat import _sse, _sse_event

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_stream_yields_delta_then_done(mock_anthropic_stream):
    """mock_anthropic_stream fixture patches AsyncAnthropic.messages.stream
    to yield ['Hello', ' world'] then final_message with usage."""
    from backend.routes.chat import _stream_response
    from backend.schemas import ChatRequest

    req = ChatRequest(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=100,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "hi"}],
    )
    chunks = []
    async for chunk in _stream_response(req, user_id="u1"):
        chunks.append(chunk)

    # All chunks except last are delta events
    deltas = [json.loads(c[6:]) for c in chunks[:-1] if c.startswith("data:")]
    assert all("delta" in d for d in deltas)
    assert "".join(d["delta"] for d in deltas) == "Hello world"

    # Last chunk is done=True
    last = json.loads(chunks[-1][6:])
    assert last["done"] is True
    assert "usage" in last


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_rate_limit_yields_error_event(mock_anthropic_rate_limited):
    from backend.routes.chat import _stream_response
    from backend.schemas import ChatRequest

    req = ChatRequest(model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", max_tokens=100,
                      messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "hi"}])
    chunks = []
    async for chunk in _stream_response(req, user_id="u1"):
        chunks.append(chunk)

    assert any("event: error" in c for c in chunks)
    error_data = next(c for c in chunks if c.startswith("data:") and "rate_limit" in c)
    assert json.loads(error_data[6:])["code"] == "rate_limit"

# tests/test_sse_integration.py
import pytest, json, httpx
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from backend.main import app

def test_streaming_endpoint_integration(mock_anthropic_stream, auth_headers):
    """TestClient handles StreamingResponse; collect all SSE lines."""
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        with client.stream("POST", "/v1/chat/completions",
                           json={"model": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
                                 "max_tokens": 100,
                                 "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "ping"}]},
                           headers=auth_headers) as resp:
            assert resp.status_code == 200
            assert "text/event-stream" in resp.headers["content-type"]

            lines = []
            for line in resp.iter_lines():
                if line.startswith("data:"):
                    lines.append(json.loads(line[5:]))

    assert any("delta" in l for l in lines)
    assert lines[-1].get("done") is True

Server-Sent Events give you streaming LLM output over plain HTTP with zero client-side complexity: use fetch + ReadableStream instead of EventSource to support POST with auth headers, yield SSE-formatted strings from a FastAPI async generator, emit a done sentinel to signal completion, and set X-Accel-Buffering: no on the nginx proxy to prevent response buffering.
